turns.name: WILLIAM REHNQUIST
turns.text: We'll hear argument next in No. 92-9059, Jonathan Dale Simmons v. South Carolina. Mr. Bruck, you may proceed.

turns.name: DAVID I. BRUCK
turns.text: Mr. Chief Justice, and may it please the Court: The issue in this capital case is whether the prosecution can secure a death sentence on the grounds of the defendant's future dangerousness, while at the same time concealing from the sentencing jury a crucial aspect of its noncapital sentencing alternative, namely that life imprisonment means life without parole. Future dangerousness was the primary reason advanced by the State in this case why the jury should sentence Jonathan Simmons to death. The defense acknowledged that the petitioner, by reason of his serious mental illness, was, in fact, dangerous or would be, in fact, dangerous were he released back into society. However, the defense showed, by expert and lay testimony, a likelihood that the petitioner would make a nonviolent and cooperative prison inmate. Now, the jury itself identified the crucial piece of missing information in this presentation. After 90 minutes of deliberation on the issue of penalty, the jury returned to the courtroom and asked the following question: Does the imposition of a life sentence carry with it the possibility of parole? And the trial judge responded by telling them three things: you are instructed not to consider parole; that is not a proper issue for your consideration; and life imprisonment and death sentence are to be understood in their plain and ordinary meaning. The issue in this case arises because of the fact that under South Carolina law the correct answer to the jury's question was no. Because Jonathan Simmons had prior convictions for a violent offense, under South Carolina law life meant exactly that, life without the possibility of parole. That was what the jury wanted to know and that is what the petitioner urged they be told. The State, from the very beginning of the trial, successfully impressed upon the trial judge not to tell the jury. It was obvious how critical this information was going to be and the -- it was obvious what an advantage the State would receive if alone among everybody in the courtroom, the jury alone would be the only ones not to know.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Mr. --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, Mr. Bruck, are you arguing that a State has an obligation to provide a truthful answer to any question that a jury may ask?

turns.name: DAVID I. BRUCK
turns.text: No, not at all. But here where there is absolutely no justifiable, there is no rational State interest in refusing to give the information that the jury asked for and where the information on the facts of this particular case was so crucial to a reliable and fair determination of the sentence.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, can a State say that we're not going to answer jury questions that involve collateral matters that are just going to distract the jury?

turns.name: DAVID I. BRUCK
turns.text: Certainly. However, what the alternative to death is in a case where the State is basically saying -- not basically, where the State is literally arguing to the jury this is a matter of self- defense. That was Mr. Harpootlian's jury argument. The question before you is what to do with someone who is a threat. He told the jury you -- the issue is not what made him this way. There was all this evidence of very very extreme abuse and sexual abuse and violence in this man's childhood. The State's response was the question before you is what to do with him now that he is in our midst.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Do juries or judges in South Carolina impose sentences in noncapital cases?

turns.name: DAVID I. BRUCK
turns.text: No, they do not.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, one must. I asked do juries or judges?

turns.name: DAVID I. BRUCK
turns.text: Oh, I'm sorry, excuse me. There is no jury sentencing except in one or two -- no there is, I believe, non whatsoever now.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: So supposing that in a noncapital case a jury comes in with a question after some deliberation and they want to know what the sentence is for this offense if they find the person guilty, is a State obligated to furnish that information?

turns.name: DAVID I. BRUCK
turns.text: Not by any means. And the reason for that is that the jury's task at the -- in the hypothetical you put to me is to determine guilt or innocence, without regard to what is going to happen. But in this case, in capital sentencing, the jury's task is to sentence. And, of course, the State cannot derive any comfort from the principle that sentencing issues shouldn't interfere with guilt in a proceeding which is a sentencing hearing.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: What your position really boils down to is not, as I understand it, a position about information or a position about alternatives. It boils down to the position that the jury should know the meaning of the terms, the meaning which the law gives to the terms that the jury is required to use in pronouncing the sentence. Is that the nub of it?

turns.name: DAVID I. BRUCK
turns.text: Yes. And most particularly our Gardner claim, our due process claim, is that even if the Eighth Amendment did not require an affirmative answer to that question, where the State puts the meaning of the noncapital sentencing so clearly into issue -- and we know it was an issue not only because of the evidence and the solicitor's -- the prosecutor's argument, but because of the jury's question. There surely -- leaving all this Court's Eighth Amendment jurisprudence to one side for a moment, there obviously it just isn't fair.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: What if the jury asks -- surely, you know, how severe the alternative punishment is is always an issue. Suppose the jury comes back and says do prisoners in State prisons have television sets; how big are the cells in State prisons?

turns.name: DAVID I. BRUCK
turns.text: I would submit that there is -- one quite reach -- readily reaches a de minimis level where the - -

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, I don't know that that's de minimis. The -- you know, will this man be on a chain gang or are there any chain gangs in the State anymore?

turns.name: DAVID I. BRUCK
turns.text: Um - -

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Those are very relevant questions for a jury that wants to know how severely this person is going to be punished if the capital sentence is not imposed. Why are those questions irrelevant? I don't think they're irrelevant at all.

turns.name: DAVID I. BRUCK
turns.text: I don't think they have absolutely no relevance, but I think it is within the broad range of the State's ability, under cases such as Johnson v. Texas, to channel and mold the jury's consideration of sentencing factors.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: But just not this question. The other ones can be excluded but this one can't.

turns.name: DAVID I. BRUCK
turns.text: No, that's correct. Because this issue not only goes to the retributive -- although we think the retributive importance of this life without parole information is very significant, but it pales towards -- beside the incapacitating --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: What if there's a statutory amendment pending that would eliminate parole? Is the judge supposed to tell; how does he answer?

turns.name: DAVID I. BRUCK
turns.text: That would eliminate parole --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: There is no -- parole is unavailable -- I'm sorry, that would make parole available. Parole is unavailable now, but there is a bill currently being debated. Can he tell them that?

turns.name: DAVID I. BRUCK
turns.text: I would think --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: I mean, isn't that the problem of answering them with respect to anything that --

turns.name: DAVID I. BRUCK
turns.text: I don't think the Constitution, obviously, requires that they be told. I think this is -- generally, the whole range of -- well, can we tell them this,- can we tell them that -- that is found in the State's brief, was answered 10 years ago in California v. Ramos. The Court addresses the question, is the information reliable; is it accurate?

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: But you -- you keep answering these questions in terms of information. Why don't you answer the question in terms of meaning? They want to know whether life means life or means something else, and isn't that a far stronger argument than simply the argument that there are degrees of relevance of extraneous information. This isn't a question about extraneous information; it's a question about meaning, isn't it?

turns.name: DAVID I. BRUCK
turns.text: Well, I agree with that, but I suppose the -- Justice Scalia's question could always be posed, well, it means life without parole, but then it also means that he will or won't have a TV set. I don't agree with that and I think that stretches --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: It's life in prison, but it isn't really prison.

turns.name: DAVID I. BRUCK
turns.text: Whatever. I think that stretches the concept of the meaning of a sentence beyond all bounds, but I can -- I would be surprised if my friend doesn't make the argument in a moment.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: But doesn't -- but your point is the meaning of a word.

turns.name: DAVID I. BRUCK
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Yeah.

turns.name: DAVID I. BRUCK
turns.text: Yes, that's right. And the importance of clarifying what the meaning --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, do you argue simply that you're entitled to the instruction if the jury asks?

turns.name: DAVID I. BRUCK
turns.text: No.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Is not the foundation of your argument that the defense counsel is entitled to make this argument to the jury if it chooses?

turns.name: DAVID I. BRUCK
turns.text: Absolutely. And the trial judge, at the prosecutor's urging, refused to allow precisely that argument. And you can see --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, what --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: So is the premise of that position that the jury is entitled to hear from counsel or from the court anything that is likely to be relevant to the jury's sentencing decision? Is that the working principle we have?

turns.name: DAVID I. BRUCK
turns.text: Well, turning to the Eighth Amendment, the Skipper-Locket aspect, which this Court need not reach, but if it were to reach that, I think the language from Justice Powell's opinion in McCleskey, any relevant circumstance which could cause the jury to fail to -- to decline to impose the death penalty. Now --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: So if a prosecutor wanted to put in - - or, pardon me, a defense counsel wanted to put in a videotape of what an execution looks like, he should be able to do that so the jury could see the consequences of what they're doing?

turns.name: DAVID I. BRUCK
turns.text: No. Because I think given the fact that the method of execution is constrained by the Eighth Amendment and there is no torturous or cruel form of execution permitted, I think it is within -- Lockett does not remove the power of the State to say that that is not relevant, and therefore it is not irrelevant circumstance. But it is impossible to say that the fact that Jonathan Simmons will not allow -- again, allow -- be allowed to be paroled and to roam the streets is not a relevant circumstance.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: I suppose we're struggling for the definition of what's relevant.

turns.name: DAVID I. BRUCK
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: How are we going to do that in the confines of this case, other than make it parole specific?

turns.name: DAVID I. BRUCK
turns.text: Well, of course, this case is about parole, and the Court need go no further than to say that when the State has a -- has a dangerous mentally ill defendant who it wishes to execute, it cannot urge the jury -- it cannot create a false dilemma. I think Beck is instructive, although not on point, in this area.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: But that leaves us without a guiding principle.

turns.name: DAVID I. BRUCK
turns.text: The Court has struggled with the meaning of relevant. In Skipper the State of South Carolina urged that it was not relevant that the defendant would be a well-behaved prisoner, because it went -- in the future in prison, because it went neither to his character and record nor to the circumstances of the offense. And the Court responded that that future -- likely future behavior, albeit only in prison, was relevant in the sense -- or was mitigating in the sense that it might reasonably incline the jury to be less likely to vote for a sentence of death.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Mr. Bruck, may I try to ask what it is exactly you're urging, that the defendant had a right to put in evidence about what the parole system law meant

turns.name: DAVID I. BRUCK
turns.text: I'm asking --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: What's your request here?

turns.name: DAVID I. BRUCK
turns.text: I'm asking you to hold that where parole is not an option, where State law is clear, as it is here, that the jury not have that information withheld from them. Our -- the center of our case was a request that State law be instructed to the jury by the judge.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: But if you were allowed to tell the jury in your argument that life means life, you wouldn't have a constitutional objection, I take it. Your argument is the jury must be told.

turns.name: DAVID I. BRUCK
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: But does it require the judge to tell them, or -- the judge wouldn't permit you to tell them either, right?

turns.name: DAVID I. BRUCK
turns.text: That's correct. The State moved and the judge so ruled.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: So is there a constitutional distinction that requires the judge be the one to say it, or is your point that the term "life" must be defined -- you must be allowed to define it for the jury? Whether counsel does it or the court does it is not a constitutional distinction.

turns.name: DAVID I. BRUCK
turns.text: It is the latter. So long as the defendant is able to have the jury informed, whether it be by his counsel or by the court. Now, of course, the jury might not take counsel's word for it and might very well, even had argument been allowed, come back with this question, in which case we think that the judge should have responded.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: I assume --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And that is because parole is likely to be an essential or a material or a substantial factor in the jury's decision? Is that the standard we're working with? We need a standard here. I know what you want so far as specific relief, but I'm not sure of the underlying principle.

turns.name: DAVID I. BRUCK
turns.text: Yes, I think that the -- it's -- it is difficult for me to lay down a rule, I think, that does not have some element of generality in it to propose one. And I think -- I don't know that the Court need establish or that it is possible to establish a bright-line rule which will clearly cover every conceivable fact situation.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Yes, but isn't it part of your case -- now I don't know if -- there's some evidence in the record, as I understand it, that without this instruction the jury will be acting on a false premise.

turns.name: DAVID I. BRUCK
turns.text: Absolutely, absolutely.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: So your -- whatever your standard is, it's limited to situations, as I understand it, where in the absence of an instruction the jury will be mislead.

turns.name: DAVID I. BRUCK
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And mislead, as I understand it, about the law.

turns.name: DAVID I. BRUCK
turns.text: That's correct, about the meaning --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: You sometimes speak in terms of fact, but what you want them told is what a legal term means which they have got to use in sentencing.

turns.name: DAVID I. BRUCK
turns.text: That is correct.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And some jurors may think it means life subject to parole, other jurors may think it means life without parole, and you want that legal definition given to them.

turns.name: DAVID I. BRUCK
turns.text: That is correct.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: So why don't you just limit your principle here to saying when there is a legitimate doubt meaning whether the answer is yes or no, right? And that would mean the prosecutors would be entitled to introduce in these cases the fact that if you give this individual life, it really doesn't mean life, he'll be walking the streets in 20 years, right?

turns.name: DAVID I. BRUCK
turns.text: I read Ramos --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Fair is fair.

turns.name: DAVID I. BRUCK
turns.text: Fair is fair.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: If it goes to the meaning of the , right, right.

turns.name: DAVID I. BRUCK
turns.text: Fair is fair.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Now, it is my impression -- it is --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: I just want --

turns.name: DAVID I. BRUCK
turns.text: I agree with that if it's true.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Yeah.

turns.name: DAVID I. BRUCK
turns.text: If the prosecutor can produce South Carolina law to show that, in fact --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Right, right, right.

turns.name: DAVID I. BRUCK
turns.text: -- Life doesn't really mean life, even though there's no parole. Ramos settled that a long time ago.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: It's my impression that some States exclude information about parole for the very purpose that juries will not distort the State parole system by discounting it in advance, so that knowing that a person's going to get time off for good behavior, or whatever, they up the sentence from what it otherwise would be in order to defeat the parole system.

turns.name: DAVID I. BRUCK
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: That's a perfectly rational thing for a State to do, it seems to me. And you're saying that that can't be done, because 35 years doesn't really mean 35 years if there's a parole system, or life does not really mean life if there's a parole system.

turns.name: DAVID I. BRUCK
turns.text: No, I don't say that can't be done at all. The crucial fact here is that there is no parole system for this defendant.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Isn't the crucial fact that the jury is sentencing here and the jury is normally not sentencing.

turns.name: DAVID I. BRUCK
turns.text: Well, that's true. And, of course, the information -- the legal definition that we think the jury should have had of its sentencing alternative is a legal definition known to the sentencer in 99.9999 percent of sentencing proceedings.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, what if the jury comes in with a question after deliberation and asks in what prison would a life term be served?

turns.name: DAVID I. BRUCK
turns.text: Well, I do not view that as part of the legal definition of its sentencing alternative.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, then, how do you define the legal definition of a sentencing alternative?

turns.name: DAVID I. BRUCK
turns.text: Well, South Carolina law does not provide for statutory law. The law defining the punishments does not deal with classification and institutional assignment. Now, I don't think that there is any -- if the State wants to show, through evidence or however, that there are huge security -- there are -- you know, there are some -- in other words, if a State wishes to show facts about the prison system that support its view of future dangerousness and show that the risk from the defendant is greater than simply life without parole might suggest, I see, under California v. Ramos, the Constitution providing no bar.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, could a State -- could a State -- could the prosecutor produce evidence that the place all these lifers are sent is really not a very well built prison and it would be fairly easy to break out of?

turns.name: DAVID I. BRUCK
turns.text: If that information is accurate, I think that that -- that under the -- I would be very surprised if a State supreme court would allow that sort of collateral trial, but I, frankly, have to say I don't read the Eighth Amendment, under your cases, as creating a bar. Providing -- the touchstone is accuracy and reliability, and I think -- our claim does not engage all of these factual matters.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, isn't it even narrower than that, Mr. Bruck? The touchstone you say is reliability, but also your -- part of your touchstone, as I understand your argument, is that in the absence of the instruction the jury would be acting on a false premise.

turns.name: DAVID I. BRUCK
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Now, in the Chief Justice's example it presumes, you know, one State prison in each State, and presumably there's not much doubt about where the man's going to go, so you wouldn't need the instruction.

turns.name: DAVID I. BRUCK
turns.text: That's correct.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And if they thought they were all going to spend their life in a country club somewhere, then perhaps it would be appropriate to clear up the

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Because the jurors came in and asked the question.

turns.name: DAVID I. BRUCK
turns.text: Because of the way the case was presented; because of the facts of the case; because of the manifest dangerousness of this man's record and of his mental illness; because of the prosecuted argument; because of the fact that the defense was prevented from making this crucial argument that you can protect society adequately, you can exercise the right of collective self- defense without putting him to death. And because, finally, lest there be the slightest doubt, the jury not only came back and asked the question, but when the judge gave a "don't think about that" response, where clearly the question called for a yes or no answer, and telegraphed to the jury that -- in effect, that, yes, there is parole but that's not --we think that that in any way affects our submission today. The State didn't want to get a fair hearing on this or spell out or clarify the definition of the definition of life imprisonment. The State wanted to keep the jury in the dark and the State was successful in doing exactly that. And I submit that under the Due Process Clause on the facts of this case, under the reliability prong of this Court's line on this Court's Eighth Amendment cases, and we also submit under the Lockett-Skipper line of the mitigating evidence given substantial and reasonable weight in the jury's deliberations. Because the failure to define the life imprisonment punishment rendered almost meaningless the defendant's Skipper evidence, his good behavior in prison evidence. What difference would it make if he's only going to be in prison for 5 or 0 years? We're concerned about what happens what he's paroled. And, indeed, the fact that he's a good inmate might actually, to the jury, make it more likely that he'll be paroled sooner and back on the streets. So for all of those reasons, we submit that what happened here cannot be squared with the Constitution of the United States. And I would note too that this is, in terms of what goes on around the country, a very narrow issue. Because of the States which have parole, precious few of them try to put this sort of a thumb on the scales for death. They tell the jury forthrightly, in the overwhelming majority of States, and I believe it goes down the line of them, that if life means life, that is usually part of the sentencing verdict itself, and the jury is not left in the dark and defendants are not exposed to the sort of unfairness that happened in this case. If I may, I should like to reserve the remainder of my time for rebuttal.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Very well, Mr. Bruck. Mr. Harpootlian, we'll hear from you.

turns.name: RICHARD A. HARPOOTLIAN
turns.text: Mr. Chief Justice, if it please the Court: It seems to me that the linchpin of Mr. Bruck's argument, and the fiction that we need to examine, is that throughout his argument to the lower court and his presentation here today he equates the term "ineligibility of parole" with "ineligibility of release." When the jury came back and asked when would he be eligible for parole, were they asking that limited, specific, technical term, or were they asking when will he be eligible to be released?

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Why don't we assume that they were asking what they asked, and they asked about eligibility for parole?

turns.name: RICHARD A. HARPOOTLIAN
turns.text: Well, Justice Souter, they may have asked about eligibility for parole. The requested instruction by Mr. Bruck during trial was concluded with the term that he will remain in jail for the rest of his life. In South Carolina while the Governor does not have the power to pardon, the Pardon and Parole Board does.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, maybe Mr. Bruck's suggested instruction was inaccurate in that respect, but do you take the position that the jury should not be told the meaning of a term about which it has a legitimate question?

turns.name: RICHARD A. HARPOOTLIAN
turns.text: I think the jury should be told the meaning of the term.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, do you think there's a legitimate question possible in the minds of jurors, given what most people know or think they know about the penal system, as to whether or not the term "life" includes a parole eligibility factor or whether it doesn't? Is that a legitimate question?

turns.name: RICHARD A. HARPOOTLIAN
turns.text: I think it is a legitimate not consider. It is not something that you should consider as part of your sentence.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: But the consequence of that, it seems to me, based on what you've already said, is that the jury should not be informed about the meaning of a term that it's got to use. It's got to say life or death. The jury should not be informed about the legal meaning of that term when the jury has a question about it.

turns.name: RICHARD A. HARPOOTLIAN
turns.text: They were given the meaning of the term "life." They were told --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: You mean in the last sentence of the judge's response.

turns.name: RICHARD A. HARPOOTLIAN
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Plain and ordinary meaning.

turns.name: RICHARD A. HARPOOTLIAN
turns.text: Yes, sir. "Life" --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: But isn't the problem with that that that is a -- given the premise of the question, it is a totally ambiguous answer. If you use the -- if it has the ordinary meaning in the mind, certainly, of some jurors -- and I would have been one of those jurors myself -- then it means yes, "Life" means subject to parole. If you give it a plain meaning which is not ordinary, then it would mean life does not include eligibility for parole. It seems to me that if you've got to answer the question, that wasn't much of an answer.

turns.name: RICHARD A. HARPOOTLIAN
turns.text: Well the answer was life means life. The answer was --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: No, that isn't what the answer was. The answer was you give the terms their plain and ordinary meaning --

turns.name: RICHARD A. HARPOOTLIAN
turns.text: Without considering --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And if you give it a plain meaning in a strict sense, it means life means life. If you give it a meaning in an ordinary sense to lots of jurors who think they -- who have heard about the parole system, then it would mean subject to parole. But whatever that answer was, it was not a plain answer.

turns.name: RICHARD A. HARPOOTLIAN
turns.text: But they have been told not to consider parole.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: But you're then simply, it seems to me, backtracking on your answer. You agree that they ought to know the meaning of the terms that they've got to use in sentencing. You don't dispute that.

turns.name: RICHARD A. HARPOOTLIAN
turns.text: No, sir, I don't dispute that.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And you are now saying, but they can't be told about that term.

turns.name: RICHARD A. HARPOOTLIAN
turns.text: I don't think that is a term that is necessary for them to know about in sentencing. It is a decision to be made --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: In other words when they have to make a choice between the death penalty and life imprisonment, they don't need to know the meaning of life imprisonment?

turns.name: RICHARD A. HARPOOTLIAN
turns.text: Life imprisonment, for their purposes, was defined as life imprisonment. Life --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, we're getting -- we're getting nowhere, because the question is whether they are entitled to a clear definition responsive to their question.

turns.name: RICHARD A. HARPOOTLIAN
turns.text: And I submit that they got a clear definition of that charge.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: So your answer, then, is that they were entitled to the answer, they are entitled to know the meaning of the terms they have to use, but there's no problem here because the definition they got was adequate.

turns.name: RICHARD A. HARPOOTLIAN
turns.text: They do not need to know the definition of the term "parole" to make that decision.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: But they do need to know the definition of the term "life" or "life imprisonment."

turns.name: RICHARD A. HARPOOTLIAN
turns.text: Yes, sir. And they were given that.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Okay. And you say they -- you agree that they're entitled to that and you say they got it.

turns.name: RICHARD A. HARPOOTLIAN
turns.text: Yes, sir.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Okay.

turns.name: RICHARD A. HARPOOTLIAN
turns.text: To go beyond life means life, and that's exactly what they were told, we would have to discuss pardon, work release, furlough.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Excuse me, I don't want to play with words, but that is not exactly what they were told. They were told that the terms the court had used were to be given their plain and ordinary meaning.

turns.name: RICHARD A. HARPOOTLIAN
turns.text: Yes, sir. Because to go beyond that would open up, in the South Carolina court's opinion, a can of worms.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, what you contend is that they were told that the sentence that they would impose would be a sentence of life imprisonment.

turns.name: RICHARD A. HARPOOTLIAN
turns.text: Yes, sir.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And you think they do not have to be told that there are other laws which we have in our State which would enable a person sentenced to life in prison, which is a sentence for life, to perhaps get parole, to perhaps get a pardon, to perhaps get work relief, all other different laws which have nothing to do with the meaning of the word "life."

turns.name: RICHARD A. HARPOOTLIAN
turns.text: That is correct, Justice Scalia. And in -- this Court, in a number of cases, Penry v. Lynaugh, did not define the term -- the court did not define the term deliberately. And yet this Court found that was sufficient.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: But I take it, then, that if we think or a majority of us think that the definition was inadequate, that it just did not respond to the jury's perplexity, that you would agree there would have to be a reversal here?

turns.name: RICHARD A. HARPOOTLIAN
turns.text: No, sir, I would not agree with that.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Then I don't understand what I thought was your answer to the question of whether the jury was or was not entitled to be told the definition of the terms it was using. Because if they're entitled to be told the definition and the definition is not inadequate, I would suppose it would follow that there would be a reversal.

turns.name: RICHARD A. HARPOOTLIAN
turns.text: No, sir. My position is that -- first of all, I would not -- obviously, for purposes of your hypothetical, that it would fail. I don't concede it fails. It's difficult for me to get to the logical assumption that it fails, for a number of reasons.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: But let's assume, for the sake of the hypothetical, that it does fail, there would have to be a reversal, wouldn't there?

turns.name: RICHARD A. HARPOOTLIAN
turns.text: Well, I would submit that the definitional term of "life imprisonment," if you have to define every term, if you have to parse --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: No, but you're -- with respect, I don't want to cut off your argument, but I want you to answer my question, and I think what you're doing is basically denying my premise. If we conclude that the definition was not adequate to inform them --

turns.name: RICHARD A. HARPOOTLIAN
turns.text: You --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: --Do you agree that there would have to be a reversal here?

turns.name: RICHARD A. HARPOOTLIAN
turns.text: As to the second part of the charge, if that was inadequate, that life means life, that you were to give it the plain and ordinary meaning.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Yeah, yeah.

turns.name: RICHARD A. HARPOOTLIAN
turns.text: I would not concede that because of the prior sentence which -- in which the judge indicated do not consider parole or parole --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, then it -- then you are taking the position that the jury is not entitled to know the meaning of the terms which it must use in pronouncing the sentence.

turns.name: RICHARD A. HARPOOTLIAN
turns.text: I am of the opinion --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Because you are saying, if I understand you, that they do have to use this term, but it is perfectly proper to deny them a definition of it.

turns.name: RICHARD A. HARPOOTLIAN
turns.text: The term --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Because that's what South Carolina said.

turns.name: RICHARD A. HARPOOTLIAN
turns.text: I just don't think parole is part of the definition of the term --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: I'm not talking about parole. I'm talking about the meaning of life imprisonment.

turns.name: RICHARD A. HARPOOTLIAN
turns.text: No, what I'm saying is --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Do they have to be given the definition of life imprisonment if they're going to use that as a sentencing alternative?

turns.name: RICHARD A. HARPOOTLIAN
turns.text: I think that they have to be given -- they have to have a definition of life. I just don't think parole is part of that. And I think that the first sentence excludes parole from that.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Okay, so we're back to the question. If the definition they were given by the court is inadequate, if we assume that -- you don't, I recognize that.

turns.name: RICHARD A. HARPOOTLIAN
turns.text: Right.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And I'm not asking you to concede it. But if you assume it for the sake of argument, then we get a reversal here.

turns.name: RICHARD A. HARPOOTLIAN
turns.text: If one of the two sentences is misunderstood, there's a reasonable likelihood of misunderstanding under Boyde, yes, sir.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Okay.

turns.name: RICHARD A. HARPOOTLIAN
turns.text: If we have not met the Boyde -- the Boyde v. California standard.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: But you say it's legally irrelevant that there are, in fact, two different kinds of life sentences, life with parole and life without parole, that that's legally irrelevant?

turns.name: RICHARD A. HARPOOTLIAN
turns.text: That is correct.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Mr. Harpootlian, do most States that have a system of life without parole tell the juries that it's life imprisonment without parole?

turns.name: RICHARD A. HARPOOTLIAN
turns.text: There are a variety -- different States have different schemes, if you will. Pursuant to this Court's decision in Ramos, the States felt that they could develop different schemes. And a number --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: It was my understanding that most States that have adopted a life imprisonment without parole go ahead and advise juries if a jury is sentencing --

turns.name: RICHARD A. HARPOOTLIAN
turns.text: Many --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: -- That it's life imprisonment without parole.

turns.name: RICHARD A. HARPOOTLIAN
turns.text: Many do, but many have a specific verdict by the jury of life without parole.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: How many States would do as South Carolina and refuse to clarify that?

turns.name: RICHARD A. HARPOOTLIAN
turns.text: There are three.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And they are?

turns.name: RICHARD A. HARPOOTLIAN
turns.text: Virginia, South Carolina, North Carolina -- and Pennsylvania, I'm sorry.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Pennsylvania instead of North Carolina or in addition?

turns.name: RICHARD A. HARPOOTLIAN
turns.text: Yes, sir, I'm sorry. I would submit to the Court that to expand the Eighth Amendment to include the custody status, if you will, of a defendant or a petitioner such as this, strains the use of the Eighth Amendment. Now, in Skipper this Court ruled that future adaptability to prison was relevant to the sentencing authority's decision. And to that end, we go back to the dangerous, number one. Number two, we have the Skipper adaptability --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: All the more reason to tell the jury that he's going to be confined’.

turns.name: RICHARD A. HARPOOTLIAN
turns.text: Well, the Skipper adaptability evidence was introduced by petitioner and so my position is that pursuant to the argument taken in context with the judge's charge, and argument by defense counsel where, on Joint Appendix page 125, petitioner's argument was that he's 22 years of age, a life sentence is punishment. The petitioner in his argument talked about life being life, the jury, I submit, as the South Carolina Supreme Court found in their decision in this case, understood life to mean life. And so the argument has to be in the context of what the jury was told, not the hypothetical that Your Honor submits for my examination.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, wasn't the jury also told by the use of a phrase that described the prisoner as being "among us?" I seem to recall that quotation somewhere in the briefs. Was that phrase used in describing him?

turns.name: RICHARD A. HARPOOTLIAN
turns.text: Yes, sir, in the context -- in this context. The argument was that the defense has provided testimony to you that he was abused as a child, that he underwent a number of difficulties as a child, that he suffers from a series of stimuli, if you will, over his young age that made him what he is today. The argument was that the question isn't how he got here. He's here,- what do we do with him now that he is here amongst us in our midst. The question is is the enormity of the crime, that is his personal characteristics, his record, his personal characteristics, enough -- and I submit it was -- for him to be sentenced to the ultimate punishment of death.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, doesn't the description of him as being amongst us suggest a different answer to Justice Kennedy's question?

turns.name: RICHARD A. HARPOOTLIAN
turns.text: No, sir, I don't think so.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: I mean it's -- the argument is irrelevant unless it is assumed that he will at some point also continue to be amongst us.

turns.name: RICHARD A. HARPOOTLIAN
turns.text: Well, even if you were to assume that he was parole eligible, as apparently the doesn't mean he's going to be paroled. There is no conclusion that he is going to be out. Even if you assume, that's a decision that will be made by someone -- again, the equation here is that -- not only that parole ineligibility means he won't be released, but parole eligibility means that he will.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: But, I thought you had conceded at the outset in your answer to my first question that if the prosecutor did make an argument about prevention of future crimes, that counsel -- defense counsel could respond to that?

turns.name: RICHARD A. HARPOOTLIAN
turns.text: That he could -- that if he argued future crimes against society in general, yes, I think so, but that wasn't done in this case.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: So if -- so if we disagree with you in our interpretation of what the jury might fairly infer from the prosecutor's argument, then we must reverse on that basis?

turns.name: RICHARD A. HARPOOTLIAN
turns.text: No, sir, again I would not concede that reversal is necessary if you find that there was some talk about future dangerousness beyond the penal institutions. I would suggest that, again, one -- you're

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Because that's what the State supreme court has, in effect, done.

turns.name: RICHARD A. HARPOOTLIAN
turns.text: What the State of South Carolina Supreme Court has done is asked the jury not to consider any -- not just parole, but any other extraneous sentencing factor.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, I understand. But with specific reference to this precise point, it is the law of the State that the jury may not be advised of this -- given this information.

turns.name: RICHARD A. HARPOOTLIAN
turns.text: That is correct.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And the reason for it is that they would therefore be less likely to impose the death sentence, isn't that what the history teaches us?

turns.name: RICHARD A. HARPOOTLIAN
turns.text: No, sir. No, sir, I think not.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: That history didn't come from supreme court conferences. I assume it came from the legislature.

turns.name: RICHARD A. HARPOOTLIAN
turns.text: It came from newspaper articles.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: From newspaper articles. And was this rule imposed by the legislature or is it one adopted by the court?

turns.name: RICHARD A. HARPOOTLIAN
turns.text: One adopted by the court.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Is there any sentence in South unsupervised furloughs at the direction or the Commission of the South Carolina Department of Corrections, for a weekend, for a holiday. And Mr. Batson confirms that in his testimony. Use of convict labor on State house grounds; he could be working on the State house grounds. Now, if we are going -- if the question is when could he be released, then all these answers must be given, I submit. Our State supreme court, following the direction given in State -- in the Ramos --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, you can't say "must" because, as Justice O'Connor pointed out, most States that have life without parole do inform the jury of that. And as I understand it, they don't go on to suggest what might be if prison regulations were this way or that way.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, of course --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: So it's not -- it can't be a must.

turns.name: RICHARD A. HARPOOTLIAN
turns.text: Well, I submit, Justice Ginsburg, the -- I agree with you, it should not be, it would not be a must. But the Supreme Court of the State of South Carolina has indicated that if we're going to consider one release mechanism, that it would open the door to the consideration of other release mechanisms.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Her point is that if it must be provided under coercion, under the coercion of some principle, that principle would require that other things with the whole truth coming out in all the cases.

turns.name: RICHARD A. HARPOOTLIAN
turns.text: I think that if I could get in front of a jury and read this list of a dozen programs that would make him eligible for release, that we would have no more life sentences in the State of South Carolina. And that is the principle that the State of South Carolina, through its supreme court, has adopted.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And taking just parole alone, if I understood you correctly earlier, under recent statutory amendments in South Carolina you think that there are more cases in which the alternative to death will be life with parole than in which it will be life without parole?

turns.name: RICHARD A. HARPOOTLIAN
turns.text: There is no question in my mind about that, no question. Many more, many, many, many more. And I would submit that the result will be that -- in South Carolina, if we get into the release mechanisms -- not just parole, but if we get into those release mechanisms, I can assure you as a prosecutor, having appeared before juries many, many times on these kinds of cases, that the jury then will discount the value of a life sentence. Thank you very much.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Thank you, Mr. Harpootlian. Mr. Bruck, you have 6 minutes remaining.

turns.name: DAVID I. BRUCK
turns.text: Mr. Chief Justice, I rise only to clarify a couple of factual items. Mr. Harpootlian, no doubt inadvertently, misdescribed the requested instruction made by the defendant. The request is at page -- pages 21 and 22 of the Joint Appendix, and it is simply that the judge read the no parole statute to the jury. As an alternative when that was denied, we then requested, as very much second or third best, a more vague instruction about that he actually will be sentenced to serve life in the State penitentiary. But that was not the request we made and that is not the request upon which this case turns.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Let's assume you had gotten what you asked for. At least to the extent that you asked for it, would you take the bitter with the sweet and concede that it would be appropriate to read some more instructions to the jury, some more statements of law to the jury, going into questions -- the possibilities of furlough and work release and so on?

turns.name: DAVID I. BRUCK
turns.text: Providing those are accurate, I think under the Eighth Amendment, for purposes of this morning, yes. I don't think the South Carolina Supreme Court will necessarily allow these -- what are, in fact, very speculative and remote possibilities. The record, as
